In 2026, Apex Inc. asks Lena to assist it in a trade-mark registration for a new product line — a matter that has nothing to do with commercial real estate, leases, or NovaCo's business.
call_of_question: Under Rule 3.4-10, does a former-client conflict prevent Lena from acting for Apex Inc. in the trade-mark registration matter?

options.A: Yes — acting for any new client while a former client still exists is automatically a conflict under Rule 3.4-10.
options.B: Yes — Lena represented NovaCo in a commercial transaction, so any commercial legal work she does for any other client is a related matter.
options.C: No — acting against a former client in a completely unrelated matter does not, by itself, create a conflict under Rule 3.4-10; the test requires that the new matter be the same as or related to the prior matter and that relevant confidential information was received; a trade-mark registration for a different company on an unrelated subject creates no former-client conflict based on the prior lease retainer alone.
options.D: No — because NovaCo consented to Lena accepting new clients when it retained her in 2023.

explanation: Under Rule 3.4-10 bars a lawyer from acting against a former client only in the same or a related matter where confidential information from the prior retainer is relevant. Where the new matter is entirely unrelated to the prior representation — different client, different subject matter, no shared confidential information — there is no former-client conflict. Lena's prior lease work for NovaCo does not disqualify her from doing trade-mark work for a different company on an unrelated matter. The rule does not impose a lifetime bar on acting against all former clients in any matter.
why_A_wrong: Incorrect. Rule 3.4-10 does not create an absolute bar on acting whenever a former client exists. The bar applies only to the same or related matters involving relevant confidential information.
why_B_wrong: Incorrect. 'Commercial transaction' is too broad a characterization to constitute a 'related matter.' The relatedness test looks at whether the confidential information from the prior retainer could be used against the former client in the new matter — here, it plainly cannot.
why_C_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Acting against a former client in a completely unrelated matter does not, by itself, create a conflict under Rule 3.4-10; the test requires that the new matter be the same as or related to the prior matter and that relevant confidential information was received; a trade-mark registration for a different company on an unrelated subject creates no former-client conflict based on the prior lease retainer alone.
why_D_wrong: Incorrect. Former-client conflicts are not waived by an implied or express consent given at the time of an earlier retainer. The analysis depends on the actual scope of the conflict, not historical consent.

source_reference.subject: Professional Responsibility
source_reference.heading: Conflicts of Interest
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Rules of Professional Conduct (LSO), Rule 3.4-10 (same or related matter — unrelated matter does not create conflict)

pr_angle.pr_issue_type: former client conflict — unrelated matter — no conflict

tested_concepts: Rule 3.4-10 — no bar on acting against former client in completely unrelated matter
same or related matter test — unrelated matter fails the test
Rule 3.4-10 does not create a lifetime absolute bar

subject: professional_responsibility
subtopic_heading: Ethical Screens — Migrating Lawyers and Firm-Wide Conflicts
fact_pattern: Lawyer Petra joins a new law firm, BrightLaw LLP. At her previous firm, Petra acted for DeltaCorp in a commercial dispute against Omega Ltd. BrightLaw currently represents Omega Ltd. in the same dispute. BrightLaw's managing partner says the firm can resolve the conflict by implementing an ethical screen around Petra — she will have no involvement in the Omega file, will be physically separated from the Omega team, will receive no financial benefit from the Omega matter, and will be instructed not to discuss the matter with anyone at the firm. BrightLaw proposes to put the screen in place immediately upon Petra's arrival.
call_of_question: Which of the following best describes the conditions under which an ethical screen may be effective for a migrating lawyer in Ontario under the Rules of Professional Conduct?

options.A: Ethical screens are never effective in Ontario — a law firm cannot cure a migrating lawyer's conflict through any internal arrangement.
options.B: An ethical screen may be effective if it is erected immediately upon the lawyer's arrival at the new firm, the conflicted lawyer is fully excluded from the matter and receives no financial benefit from it, no confidential information has been shared, and the screen genuinely prevents any transfer of information; the screen must be timely, rigorous, and genuine.
options.C: An ethical screen is always effective for migrating lawyers — any internal barrier completely resolves all conflicts brought from a prior firm.
options.D: An ethical screen is effective only if the former client (DeltaCorp) provides written consent to the screen arrangement.

explanation: Under the LSO Rules of Professional Conduct recognize that ethical screens can, in some circumstances, resolve conflicts arising from a lawyer migrating from one firm to another. The conditions for an effective screen include: the screen must be erected immediately upon the lawyer's arrival (not after confidential information has already been shared); the conflicted lawyer must be completely excluded from the matter; the conflicted lawyer must receive no financial benefit from the conflicted retainer; no confidential information may have transferred before the screen was established; and the screen must be genuine and rigorously maintained. Screens are not effective in all circumstances — if confidential information was already transmitted, or if the screen is implemented late or imperfectly, it will not cure the conflict.
why_A_wrong: Incorrect. Ethical screens are recognized in Ontario as a potential mechanism to resolve conflicts from migrating lawyers in appropriate circumstances. They are not categorically ineffective.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. An ethical screen may be effective if it is erected immediately upon the lawyer's arrival at the new firm, the conflicted lawyer is fully excluded from the matter and receives no financial benefit from it, no confidential information has been shared, and the screen genuinely prevents any transfer of information; the screen must be timely, rigorous, and genuine.
why_C_wrong: Incorrect. Screens are not automatically effective in all circumstances. Timing, completeness, and genuine implementation are essential. A screen cannot undo confidential information already transferred.
why_D_wrong: Incorrect. While former-client consent may sometimes be sought, it is not a universal requirement for an ethical screen to be effective. The screen's effectiveness turns on the conditions of its implementation, not exclusively on consent.

source_reference.subject: Professional Responsibility
source_reference.heading: Conflicts of Interest
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Rules of Professional Conduct (LSO), Rule 3.4 Commentary — ethical screens and migrating lawyers

tested_concepts: ethical screen conditions — immediate, complete, no information transfer, no financial benefit
screens are not universally effective — timing and completeness matter
screen cannot cure conflict where confidential information already transferred

subject: professional_responsibility
subtopic_heading: Consenting to a Conflict — Requirements for Valid Consent — Rule 3.4-5
fact_pattern: Lawyer Orla discloses a conflict of interest to her client, Remy, in a short email: 'I want to let you know there is a conflict. I can still act for you. Please sign below if you agree.' Remy signs the email and returns it. Orla proceeds to act for Remy. Later, Remy complains that he did not understand the nature or extent of the conflict or its potential impact on his case when he signed the email.
call_of_question: Was Remy's consent a valid consent to the conflict under Rule 3.4-5?

options.A: Yes — Remy signed the document confirming his consent; written consent is always valid regardless of what was disclosed.
options.B: No — for consent to a conflict to be valid under Rule 3.4-5, the client must be fully informed of the nature and extent of the conflict, must understand the material risks of the representation proceeding despite the conflict, and must provide consent in writing after receiving that disclosure; a vague email that does not explain the nature or impact of the conflict does not constitute adequate disclosure for valid consent.
options.C: Yes — Orla made written disclosure and obtained written consent; the Rules require nothing more.
options.D: No — consent to a conflict can never be obtained by email; it must be provided in person before a commissioner of oaths.

explanation: Under Rules of Professional Conduct (LSO), Rule 3.4-5 (informed consent — requirements for valid consent), Valid consent to a conflict under Rule 3.4-5 requires more than a client's signature on a document — it requires that the client be truly informed. The lawyer must disclose the specific nature of the conflict, its extent, the material risks of proceeding, and the implications for the client's representation. Disclosure must be sufficiently specific that a reasonable client in the same position could make a genuinely informed decision. A vague email that says only 'there is a conflict' and 'I can still act for you' fails this standard. If the client does not understand what is being consented to, the consent is not informed and therefore not valid.
why_A_wrong: Incorrect. A client's signature on a document does not, without more, constitute valid consent. The consent must be informed — meaning the client must understand the nature and extent of the conflict before signing.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. For consent to a conflict to be valid under Rule 3.4-5, the client must be fully informed of the nature and extent of the conflict, must understand the material risks of the representation proceeding despite the conflict, and must provide consent in writing after receiving that disclosure; a vague email that does not explain the nature or impact of the conflict does not constitute adequate disclosure for valid consent.
why_C_wrong: Incorrect. The Rules require adequate substantive disclosure, not merely a form of writing. The content of the disclosure — the nature, extent, and risks of the conflict — is what makes consent valid, not the medium.
why_D_wrong: Incorrect. There is no requirement that consent to a conflict be given in person before a commissioner of oaths. Written consent through appropriate means (including email) is permissible, provided it follows adequate disclosure.

source_reference.subject: Professional Responsibility
source_reference.heading: Conflicts of Interest
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Rules of Professional Conduct (LSO), Rule 3.4-5 (informed consent — requirements for valid consent)

tested_concepts: Rule 3.4-5 — valid consent requires full disclosure of nature, extent, and risks
signature alone does not constitute informed consent
vague disclosure is insufficient for valid conflict consent
